question: What is the metaverse often described as in the document?
choices: A single video game made by one company
A collective virtual shared space combining physical and digital realities
A new brand of VR headset
A simple 2D chat room on the internet

question: Which technology is most closely associated with accessing the metaverse?
choices: Virtual reality headsets and immersive interfaces
Traditional television remotes
Fax machines
USB flash drives

question: According to the student sheet, which company rebranded itself as 'Meta' to focus on the metaverse?
choices: Apple
Microsoft
Facebook
Google

question: What is one potential benefit of the metaverse mentioned in the document?
choices: Limiting communication to text messages only
New forms of social interaction, work, and entertainment in immersive worlds
Reducing access to online education
Eliminating all forms of remote collaboration

question: Which concern about the metaverse is highlighted in the text?
choices: Too much paper being used
Increased risks for privacy and data collection
Lack of internet connections globally
Too few companies investing in technology

question: What happens to your 'avatar' in the metaverse?
choices: It is only used to send emails
It represents you as a digital character or presence in virtual spaces
It replaces your physical body in the real world
It is always controlled by the platform, not by you

question: Which of the following is mentioned as a possible use of the metaverse in education or work?
choices: Attending 3D virtual classes or meetings with other avatars
Sending physical letters faster
Replacing all books with radio
Blocking video conferencing

question: What warning is given about spending time in the metaverse?
choices: People might become too good at sports
There is a risk of spending more time in virtual worlds than in real life
It forces people to live outdoors
It automatically limits your screen time

question: Which economic aspect of the metaverse is mentioned in the student document?
choices: The metaverse will remove all jobs
Virtual economies where users can buy, sell, or create digital goods
A total ban on digital payments
Only governments can own virtual land

question: Which expression from the sheet is closest in meaning to 'being constantly surrounded by digital content'?
choices: Digital immersion
Physical isolation
Paper overload
Network blackout

question: Which potential danger related to behaviour is raised in the document?
choices: The fear of learning foreign languages
Harassment or toxic behaviour transferring into virtual worlds
Overuse of paper notebooks
Shortage of video games

question: What question from the worksheet invites students to think critically about identity in the metaverse?
choices: "How many VR games do you own?"
"Would you behave differently behind an avatar than in real life?"
"What is your favourite console?"
"How often do you watch TV?"

question: Which risk linked to companies in the metaverse is mentioned?
choices: Companies will stop using the internet
Big tech firms could gain even more control over users' data and experiences
Only small start‑ups can create content
No adverts will exist in virtual spaces

question: Which of these is a possible advantage of the metaverse for social life, according to the sheet?
choices: Meeting people only by email
Connecting with friends or communities all over the world in shared virtual spaces
Avoiding all contact with others
Blocking real‑time communication

question: What type of question does the teacher ask to start a debate on the metaverse?
choices: If the metaverse becomes common, will it improve or damage real‑life relationships?
How many likes do you have on Instagram?
What is the price of your smartphone?
Which cinema do you prefer?
